evidence_quotes.quote: Hundreds of games have already been taken offline due to no end of life plans AFTER the product has been sold to the customer.
One of their executives has infamously said that "Gamers need to get comfortable with not owning their games", i.e. not owning the product you have paid for. They are essentially selling a lease.

paragraphs.text: Greetings. As a citizen of an EU candidate country of Serbia, I would like to say that some video game publishers are not adhering to the Digital Fairness Act, or, at the very least, they are borderline trying to challenge it. Hundreds of games have already been taken offline due to no end of life plans AFTER the product has been sold to the customer. The Stop Killing Games initiative (Known as "Stop Destroying Games" EU citizens initiative, an initiative which has gotten over 1.4 million signatures) has a dead game list on their website which you can reference for the exact number of games being taken offline. If possible, explore the page; a lot of information on hundreds of video games that are dead or are at risk of dying are on there. I will be attaching a word document containing a screenshot (png/jpeg files are not a valid file format) in which you can see that a game I have personally paid for (and that ended up being the catalyst for the Stop Killing/Destroying Games initiative starting), The Crew, marked as "inactive", despite me paying for it, and despite the game having an offline mode. The game has a single player campaign (about 20 hours of content, refer to the game's wikipedia page for verification) and a multiplayer mode. Ubisoft, the publisher, simply decided to take the entire game away from people who have paid for it instead of allowing it to exist, they didn't even bother to allow the single player campaign to remain playable, the entire game is just... Gone. The product people have bought, gone in an instant, despite it being possible to preserve it. Even if Ubisoft didn't want to keep paying for servers for the online mode, there is, in my opinion, absolutely no excuse for not preserving the single player campaign for their customers. Ubisoft are not the only company to act this way. EA and Activision also have a history of similar behaviour, but Ubisoft is particularly egregious with the way they treat their customers. One of their executives has infamously said that "Gamers need to get comfortable with not owning their games", i.e. not owning the product you have paid for. They are essentially selling a lease, but when the game is being marketed it is treated like a normal purchase. Something needs to be done about this. Multi billion euro companies are actively taking advantage of consumers, all in the name of, presumably, saving money. That is the only conclusion I can conceivably come to, why else would they be refusing to preserve their product they have profited off of if they are not just looking at these games as a means to an end? When/if Serbia ever joins the EU, I would like for the consumer rights laws to be robust and hold people accountable if they do not adhere to them. I really wish that I could sign the Stop Destroying Games initiative, but alas, that is not possible, so this feedback will be my small contribution to the cause I greatly believe in. Hopefully the EU resonates with the concerns of millions of gamers, EU and worldwide. Sincerely, Branko.
